skill.name: appinsights-instrumentation
skill.description: Guidance for instrumenting Azure-hosted web applications with Application Insights. It explains SDK setup, telemetry configuration, resource creation options, and connection string handling.
skill.summary: Plan Application Insights instrumentation for Azure web applications.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis raised a critical heuristic because documentation contains command snippets, Azure portal links, and environment variable examples. Review found these are expected Application Insights guidance, with no prompt injection, obfuscation, hidden execution, or data exfiltration evidence. Risk is low because users may run cloud configuration commands, but the skill itself is documentation-oriented and publishable.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines describe Application Insights resources, resource metadata, or best practices. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text contains descriptions and Azure resource fields only. There is no hashing, cipher selection, TLS downgrade, or custom cryptography.

low_findings.title: Command Snippets Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to package install commands, SDK setup snippets, and markdown examples. They are not executed by the skill and do not build commands from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear inside markdown guidance and fenced examples. I found no executable wrapper, dynamic command construction, or automatic invocation path.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Are Not Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts are relative markdown links to a local script reference. They do not read arbitrary files or traverse user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both instances are static markdown links using a fixed relative path. No filesystem API or user-controlled path joins are present.

low_findings.title: Azure Portal URL Template Is Expected Guidance
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to Azure Portal documentation and a portal blade template. It does not send secrets to an external collection endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is an Azure Portal navigation template for user-driven setup. No network call is made by the skill content.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Use Handles Expected Telemetry Configuration
low_findings.description: The environment variable examples reference the Application Insights connection string. This is normal SDK configuration and not evidence of secret theft.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable is consumed by Azure Monitor SDK setup. I found no code that prints, stores, or transmits the value outside the intended SDK path.

low_findings.title: Composite Heuristic Does Not Indicate Malicious Intent
low_findings.description: The critical combined-pattern alert results from benign documentation categories appearing together. No prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or hidden automation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combined signals map to normal observability setup guidance. No semantic evidence supports malicious intent after reviewing the referenced files.

content.user_title: Instrument Azure Apps with Application Insights
content.value_statement: Teams need clear telemetry setup guidance before changing production applications. This skill explains Application Insights instrumentation choices, SDK setup, and connection string configuration.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Azure App Service auto-instrumentation may fit an application.
Shows Application Insights and Log Analytics resource planning with Bicep guidance.
Provides SDK setup guidance for ASP.NET Core, Node.js, and Python applications.
Explains how to configure APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING for hosted applications.
Summarizes OpenTelemetry SDK practices for TypeScript, Python, and Java references.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples; it does not safely perform project changes by itself.
Azure subscription, resource group, and hosting details must come from the user or workspace context.
The strongest coverage is for Azure-hosted web applications, especially App Service scenarios.
It does not replace production observability design, cost review, or privacy review.

use_cases.title: Choose an instrumentation path
use_cases.description: Compare auto-instrumentation and manual SDK setup before changing an Azure-hosted web app.

use_cases.title: Plan monitoring infrastructure
use_cases.description: Identify the Application Insights and Log Analytics resources needed for a deployment template.

use_cases.title: Review telemetry setup requirements
use_cases.description: Check package, environment variable, and deployment requirements before approving telemetry work.

prompt_templates.title: Identify my app type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me identify whether my Azure web app should use Application Insights auto-instrumentation or manual SDK setup.

prompt_templates.title: Plan ASP.NET Core telemetry
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the Application Insights setup steps for an ASP.NET Core app hosted in Azure App Service.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare environment configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me what information I need to configure APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING for my hosted application.

prompt_templates.title: Design a multi-language rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a rollout plan for Application Insights across ASP.NET Core, Node.js, and Python services using OpenTelemetry guidance.

output_examples.input: Can my Azure App Service use auto-instrumentation?
output_examples.output: A short decision checklist covering hosting type, supported runtime, portal setup, and when manual SDK setup is better.

output_examples.input: What do I need before adding Application Insights to Node.js?
output_examples.output: A setup summary covering the Azure Monitor package, early SDK initialization, connection string configuration, and deployment notes.

output_examples.input: How should I plan infrastructure for telemetry?
output_examples.output: A resource plan describing Application Insights, Log Analytics workspace use, naming, region choice, and environment variable configuration.

best_practices: Confirm runtime, framework, and Azure hosting model before choosing an instrumentation approach.
Store the Application Insights connection string in managed application settings or deployment configuration.
Deploy telemetry changes through the normal release process and verify data in Azure Monitor.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode connection strings in application source files.
Do not modify production telemetry settings without confirming resource group and app identity.
Do not use manual SDK setup when supported auto-instrumentation meets the application requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill add Application Insights to my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Use an implementation workflow for actual project changes.

faq.question: Which application types are covered best?
faq.answer: The guidance focuses on Azure-hosted ASP.NET Core and Node.js apps, with additional Python and SDK references.

faq.question: Does it support Azure App Service auto-instrumentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when portal-based auto-instrumentation can be used for supported App Service applications.

faq.question: How should the connection string be stored?
faq.answer: Use application settings, environment variables, or deployment configuration. Do not place secrets in source code.

faq.question: Can it help with Bicep templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for adding Application Insights and Log Analytics resources to infrastructure templates.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples and normal telemetry configuration, not malicious execution or exfiltration.
